profiles.label: Balanced
profiles.description: Equal weighting across all domains — suitable for general assessments and organisations without specific regulatory constraints.

profiles.label: Financial Services
profiles.description: Heavily weighted towards data residency, assurance, and executive governance driven by financial regulation.

profiles.label: Healthcare
profiles.description: Strong emphasis on data protection and assurance due to patient data sensitivity and healthcare compliance mandates.

profiles.label: Government & Public Sector
profiles.description: Highest priority on data sovereignty, assurance, and executive commitment — sovereignty is a constitutional requirement.

profiles.label: Technology & SaaS
profiles.description: Prioritises technical independence and open-source strategy as core competitive and risk-management levers.

profiles.label: Manufacturing & Industrial
profiles.description: Operational resilience and managed service control are critical for industrial continuity and supply chain integrity.

profiles.label: Telecommunications
profiles.description: Data, operational resilience, and assurance are critical given telecoms infrastructure and regulatory exposure.

profiles.label: Energy & Utilities
profiles.description: Operational continuity and managed service control are paramount for critical national infrastructure.

domains.title: Data Sovereignty
domains.description: Data control, residency, and encryption sovereignty

domains.title: Managed Services
domains.description: Cloud service control and provider independence

maturityLevels.name: Initial
maturityLevels.description: Unpredictable, poorly controlled, reactive processes. Digital sovereignty is not yet a managed practice in your organisation.

actions.title: Assess Current State
actions.detail: Conduct a baseline inventory of data assets, cloud dependencies, and third-party suppliers.

actions.title: Identify Critical Gaps
actions.detail: Pinpoint the highest-risk sovereignty exposures in data storage, access, and jurisdiction.

actions.title: Secure Leadership Buy-in
actions.detail: Present sovereignty risks in business terms to executive leadership to gain mandate and resource.

actions.title: Quick Wins
actions.detail: Implement basic data classification, encryption at rest, and access logging as immediate controls.

actions.title: Awareness Training
actions.detail: Run sovereignty awareness sessions for technical staff and procurement teams.

maturityLevels.name: Managed
maturityLevels.description: Projects are planned and executed per policy. Basic controls are in place but not yet standardised across the organisation.

actions.title: Develop Strategy
actions.detail: Create a digital sovereignty roadmap aligned with business objectives and regulatory requirements.

actions.title: Implement Controls
actions.detail: Deploy encryption key management (BYOK/HYOK) and enforce data residency at the platform level.

actions.title: Establish Governance
actions.detail: Form a sovereignty governance committee with clear ownership, responsibilities, and escalation paths.

actions.title: Document Procedures
actions.detail: Create standard operating procedures for sovereignty-critical activities such as vendor onboarding and data transfers.

actions.title: Build Capabilities
actions.detail: Train technical teams on sovereign technologies, cloud-native security, and open-source tooling.

actions.title: Evaluate Solutions
actions.detail: Research and pilot open-source and EU-certified sovereign-ready platforms for key workloads.

maturityLevels.name: Defined
maturityLevels.description: Standardised, documented, and proactive processes exist organisation-wide. Sovereignty is an embedded practice.

actions.title: Standardise Processes
actions.detail: Roll out sovereignty controls consistently across all programmes, projects, and business units.

actions.title: Automate Compliance
actions.detail: Implement automated scanning and policy-as-code to continuously verify sovereignty posture.

actions.title: Vendor Framework
actions.detail: Establish a formal supplier sovereignty assessment programme with contractual minimum standards.

actions.title: Open Source Roadmap
actions.detail: Define and execute a strategic open-source adoption plan, replacing high-risk proprietary dependencies.

actions.title: Pursue Certification
actions.detail: Target relevant national and EU certifications such as NIS2 compliance, SecNumCloud, or BSI C5.

maturityLevels.name: Quantitatively Managed
maturityLevels.description: Sovereignty is measured and controlled using data and statistical techniques. Decisions are evidence-driven.

actions.title: Define Metrics & KPIs
actions.detail: Establish sovereignty KPIs across all domains and integrate them into executive reporting dashboards.

actions.title: Continuous Monitoring
actions.detail: Deploy real-time sovereignty posture monitoring with automated alerts for deviations.

actions.title: Quantify Risk
actions.detail: Model sovereignty risk in financial and operational terms to support investment decisions.

actions.title: Supply Chain Visibility
actions.detail: Achieve full, auditable visibility of third-party sovereignty dependencies and sub-processors.

actions.title: Advanced Auditing
actions.detail: Implement continuous compliance auditing with automated evidence collection and remediation workflows.

maturityLevels.name: Optimising
maturityLevels.description: Continuous improvement and innovation are embedded. Your organisation is a sovereignty leader and ecosystem contributor.

actions.title: Lead Innovation
actions.detail: Contribute to EU sovereignty frameworks, open-source projects, and industry working groups.

actions.title: Ecosystem Building
actions.detail: Share best practices with peers, suppliers, and public sector bodies to raise the collective baseline.

actions.title: Full Automation
actions.detail: Achieve fully automated sovereignty compliance, reporting, and evidence generation with minimal human effort.

actions.title: Future-Proof Architecture
actions.detail: Design for emerging requirements such as the EU AI Act, Data Act, and next-generation secure hardware.

actions.title: Policy Engagement
actions.detail: Engage proactively with regulators and standards bodies to shape sovereignty frameworks.

questions.text: Does your organisation currently comply with all data residency requirements or regulations relevant to your country, region, or sector?
questions.explanation: Data residency laws require certain data to be stored and processed within specific geographic boundaries. Non-compliance can result in significant regulatory penalties and loss of operating licences.

questions.text: Do you control and manage your encryption keys exclusively, without sharing them with cloud providers?
questions.explanation: If your cloud provider holds your encryption keys (provider-managed keys), they — and by extension foreign governments with legal reach over that provider — can potentially access your data. Customer-managed keys (BYOK/HYOK) give you sole control.

questions.text: Can you technically prevent sensitive data from crossing specific geographic or jurisdictional borders?
questions.explanation: Critical for compliance with data localisation laws. Requires technical controls such as geo-fenced storage policies, data egress monitoring, and contractual prohibitions to ensure data stays within approved regions.

questions.text: Can you meaningfully mitigate vendor lock-in risks with your current technology stack?
questions.explanation: Vendor lock-in occurs when proprietary technologies, APIs, or data formats make it costly or technically difficult to switch providers. Open standards, container-based workloads, and infrastructure-as-code reduce this risk significantly.

questions.text: Do you prioritise open standards over proprietary APIs when selecting or building platforms?
questions.explanation: Open, publicly governed standards (such as OCI, S3, LDAP, OAuth) mean your workloads are not tied to a single vendor's implementation. Proprietary APIs create hidden dependencies that only become visible when you try to leave.

questions.text: Could you migrate your critical applications to a different cloud platform if required?
questions.explanation: Platform portability is a practical test of technical sovereignty. If migration would take more than a few months or require architectural redesign, your dependency on the current provider is a strategic risk.

questions.text: Can you continue operating critical systems if your external cloud services become unavailable?
questions.explanation: Operational resilience means critical systems can run independently if cloud providers experience outages, revoke service, or are subject to regulatory action. This requires on-premise fallback capability or multi-cloud architectures.

questions.text: Does your organisation have in-house technical expertise to manage sovereign infrastructure without relying on the vendor?
questions.explanation: Managing sovereign systems requires specialist skills in security, compliance, and infrastructure operations. Over-reliance on vendor-provided support erodes operational sovereignty and creates a single point of dependency.

questions.text: Do your disaster recovery plans account for geopolitical scenarios such as sanctions, trade restrictions, or foreign access laws?
questions.explanation: Traditional DR focuses on technical failures. Geopolitical DR must also address scenarios where an international provider is legally compelled to restrict, suspend, or hand over access to your systems — including the CLOUD Act, Chinese Cybersecurity Law, and similar statutes.

questions.text: Do you have contractual rights to audit your vendors' security practices and controls independently?
questions.explanation: The right to audit — and the practical ability to exercise it — is the difference between taking a vendor's word for their security posture and verifying it independently. Without audit rights, assurance claims are unverifiable.

questions.text: Do you control where your security logs and audit trails are stored, and can you prevent the vendor accessing them?
questions.explanation: Security logs contain sensitive information about your systems and user behaviour. If logs are stored in vendor-controlled infrastructure, the vendor — and any government with jurisdiction over them — may be able to access your security telemetry.

questions.text: Is your organisation certified against relevant national or EU security standards such as NIS2, SecNumCloud, BSI C5, or ISO 27001?
questions.explanation: Certification against recognised standards provides independent assurance that your security controls meet a defined baseline. It also signals sovereignty commitment to customers, regulators, and procurement bodies.

questions.text: Do you have a formal policy that favours open-source software over proprietary alternatives where functionally equivalent?
questions.explanation: A formal OSS policy creates consistent decision-making criteria for procurement and architecture. Without it, proprietary solutions accumulate by default, increasing lock-in and reducing transparency.

questions.text: Could you fork and independently maintain critical open-source dependencies if the upstream project was abandoned or compromised?
questions.explanation: Forking capability is the practical safety net of open source. If your team lacks the skills or processes to maintain a fork, your sovereignty benefit from OSS is theoretical rather than real.

questions.text: Does your organisation actively contribute to open-source projects that are strategically important to your operations?
questions.explanation: Contributing to upstream projects gives your organisation influence over the project's direction, early visibility of security issues, and deeper internal expertise — all of which strengthen your long-term sovereignty position.

questions.text: Do you have a named executive sponsor or steering committee with accountability for digital sovereignty?
questions.explanation: Without executive ownership, sovereignty initiatives lack the authority to override cost-driven procurement decisions or challenge vendor proposals. A named sponsor signals organisational commitment and provides escalation authority.

questions.text: Is digital sovereignty explicitly included in your corporate or IT strategy documents?
questions.explanation: Strategy-level inclusion ensures sovereignty considerations are applied at the point of architectural and procurement decisions, not retrofitted after the fact. It also makes sovereignty a measurable objective for leadership teams.

questions.text: Is there a dedicated budget allocated specifically for digital sovereignty initiatives and compliance?
questions.explanation: Budget allocation is the clearest indicator of organisational seriousness. Without dedicated funding, sovereignty work competes with — and typically loses to — feature delivery and operational cost reduction.

questions.text: Can you restrict cloud service deployments to specific certified regions or data centres?
questions.explanation: The ability to enforce geographic and certification constraints on deployments is a basic managed services sovereignty control. Without it, workloads may inadvertently land outside approved jurisdictions during scaling or failover events.

questions.text: Do you control and actively monitor your cloud provider's administrative access to your systems?
questions.explanation: Privileged access by vendor support staff represents a high-risk sovereignty exposure. Controlling and logging when, why, and who accesses your environment — including just-in-time access models — is essential for assurance and incident response.

questions.text: Have you tested and validated your ability to migrate workloads to a different cloud provider?
questions.explanation: Theoretical portability and proven portability are very different things. A tested migration — even a partial one — reveals hidden dependencies, undocumented vendor-specific features, and operational gaps that paper assessments cannot surface.
